text: Abstract Metamaterials can be 3D printed with user defined internal structures to achieve novel mechanical properties that are not inherent to the individual constituent materials, and such mechanical properties are recorded as material response curves, with stress–strain curves representing their quasi-static (i.e., static) component. Current inverse design methods are not yet mature enough to fully reverse-engineer complex mechanical properties due to multi-objective design, nonlinearity, and 3D printing manufacturing tolerances. In this work, we present a novel, accelerated inverse design framework that merges generative machine learning with desktop 3D printing to fabricate a vast array of unrestrained uniaxial compressive stress–strain curve properties while accounting for the effects of 3D printing manufacturing tolerance uncertainties. Our results demonstrate that tailorability of material mechanical properties can be achieved while maintaining near 90% measurement fidelity between the desired and fabricated stress–strain curves. Our work not only contributes to a path toward designing materials with complex mechanical properties, but also has the potential to accelerate design and manufacturing iteration.
